subject: IDS
course_description: Variable topics using interdisciplinary approaches from the humanities and social sciences. Topics represent current interests of the instructor.
course_notes: (Freshmen Seminar - Limited to First Year Students Only) (This class examines the relationship between religion, self-care, and consumerism. We start with a simple premise; the way we take care of ourselves is political. Whether we realize it or not, many of us engage with religion in our daily lives. If you have seen Star Wars, used a yoga mat or meditation app, or attended a Burning Man festival, you have encountered religion. What does it mean when aspects of a religious tradition are transformed into consumer products? What cultural, ethical, and personal dilemmas emerge when religious ideas and objects are used for self-care separate from their religious context? Who decides which religions to borrow from, and which parts of those religions are 'extractible'? How do we approach practices like mindfulness, which have been scientifically proven to have health benefits, but are also sometimes viewed as a forms of cultural appropriation? Who can afford to participate in a consumerist wellness culture? Are there anti-capitalist forms of self-care? Can we imagine what our lives would look like if our society prioritized rest? These are all questions we will consider in this class. Students will have the opportunity to critically examine and experientially engage with contemporary self-care and wellness practices.
